Final Project

Harsimran Ghai

2026-04-17

My github link is https://github.com/harsimranghai91-eng/Final-Project-CHEP-898.git.

Introduction to the project

Myocardial infarction occurs due to decrease in blood supply to the myocardium (Heart Muscles), commonly due to obstruction in the coronary arteries. The risk factors for MI are as follows: Smoking, alcohol intake, sociodemographic factors, diet, increase level of homocysteine, diabetes, hypertension, high cholesterol, sedentary lifestyle, obesity (1). The global prevalence of MI is 3.8% for people age less than 60 and 9.5% for people greater than 60 years of age (2). Given, its global burden, it is imperative to create a predictive model that can predict the person risk of having the heart attack based on certain features.

Objectives of this study

The main objective of this study is to create different predictive models for predicting myocardial infarction (Heart attack). The best machine learning model will be selected based on different criterias (Refer to model selection in methodology section)

Methodology

dataset

Heart disease dataset from kaggle was used for this project. The link to the project is as follows:https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/ankushpanday2/heart-attack-risk-dataset-of-china. This dataset is based on chinese population and consist of 239266 observations and consist of 28 features (Predictors).

Statistical methods

machine Learning models

Four different classification machine learning models was created that is logistic regression, logistic regression with up-sampling, random forest and random forest with up- sampling. The reason behind using random forest rather than decision tree is that decision tree even though easy to interpret tend to underperform in comparison to logistics regression models, that why ensemble method (random forest) is used. Additionally, the reason behind using up-sampling is the vast proportion difference of people with heart attack and without heart attack in this dataset.The up-sampling tend to balance such difference.

The recipe was created such that variables that are irrelevant such as patientID, province, and hypertension status were removed. The hypertension was removed as we have continuous blood pressure measurements which improves the power of the model in comparison to using only hypertension categorical variable. Listwise deletion was perform to remove observations with missing variables information. Additionally,the dummy coding of the categorical variables that have more than two levels was done. Further, predictors with non zero variance and numeric predictor that are highly related were deleted (if any).

Validation of the models

The data was split into train and testing dataset. To find the optimal hyperparameters, 10- fold cross validation was used where the training data was split into five fold. In each iteration, the model was fit on the nine folds with the remaining fold used as the validation set. This approach ensures that the each folds act as the validation set exactly once. Once the hyperparameters are known, the model is fitted on the entire training data and its predictive performance tested on the testing data.

Model comparison

The performance of each model was compared using the following criteria : Accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F1 Score. The model with the high score for the parameters will be selected as the best predictive model.

Variable Information

Dependent Variable

  1. Heart attack
    Binary: “Yes” = no heart attack, “No” = heart attack

Independent Variables

  1. Age – Continuous
  2. Blood Pressure – Continuous
  3. CVD Risk Score – Continuous
  4. Hypertension – Binary
  5. Diabetes – Binary
  6. Obesity – Binary
  7. Cholesterol – Categorical (High, Normal, Low)
  8. Air Pollution Exposure – Categorical (High, Medium, Low)
  9. Physical Activity – Categorical (High, Medium, Low)
  10. Diet Score – Categorical (Poor, Moderate, Healthy)
  11. Stress Level – Categorical (Low, Medium, High)
  12. Alcohol Consumption – Binary
  13. Family History CVD – Binary
  14. Healthcare Access – Categorical (Poor, Moderate, Good)
  15. Rural or Urban – Binary
  16. Hospital Availability – Categorical (Low, Medium, High)
  17. TCM Use – Binary
  18. Employment Status – Categorical (Employed, Unemployed, Retired)
  19. Education Level – Categorical (None, Primary, Secondary, Higher)
  20. Income Level – Categorical (Low, Middle, High)
  21. Chronic Kidney Disease – Binary
  22. Previous Heart Attack – Binary

Data Wrangling

Loading the Data

data<-read.csv("heart_attack_china.csv")

Changing the Structure of variables

data <- data%>%mutate(across(c( Gender,
                                Smoking_Status,
                                Hypertension,
                                Diabetes,
                                Obesity,
                                Cholesterol_Level,
                                Air_Pollution_Exposure,
                                Physical_Activity,
                                Diet_Score,
                                Stress_Level,
                                Alcohol_Consumption,

                                Healthcare_Access,
                                Rural_or_Urban,
                                Hospital_Availability,
                                TCM_Use,
                                Employment_Status,
                                Education_Level,
                                Income_Level,
                                Chronic_Kidney_Disease,
                                Previous_Heart_Attack,
                                Heart_Attack
                            
),~ as.factor(.)))

    x = "Heart Attack",
    y = "CVD Risk Scores",

  theme_minimal()

Machine Learning Models

Logistic Regression

Loading the packages

library(tidyverse)
library(tidymodels)
library(sjPlot)
library(finalfit)
library(knitr)
library(gtsummary)
library(mlbench)
library(vip)
library(rsample)
library(tune)
library(recipes)
library(yardstick)
library(parsnip)
library(glmnet)
library(themis)
library(microbenchmark)

Data Split

set.seed(10)

data_split <- initial_split(data, prop = 0.70, strata = Heart_Attack)

train_data <- training(data_split)
table(train_data$Heart_Attack)
## 
##     No    Yes 
## 147136  20349
test_data  <- testing(data_split)
table(test_data$Heart_Attack)
## 
##    No   Yes 
## 63059  8722

The data is split into training and testing datasets using 70-30 splitting rule.

Creating 10-fold splits

set.seed(10)

folds <- vfold_cv(train_data, v = 10, strata= Heart_Attack)

Setting the reference of dependent variable

train_data$Heart_Attack <- relevel(factor(train_data$Heart_Attack), ref = "No")

Defining logistic regression model

logistic_model <- logistic_reg() %>%
        set_engine("glm", control= list(maxit=200)) %>%
        set_mode("classification")

Creating recipe

rec <- recipe(Heart_Attack ~ ., data = train_data) %>%
  step_rm(Patient_ID, Province, Hypertension) %>%  
  step_naomit(all_predictors(), skip = TRUE) %>%
  step_nzv(all_predictors()) %>%  
  step_corr(all_numeric_predictors(), threshold = 0.9) %>% 
  step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors()) %>%
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())

Creating a workflow

wf <- workflow() %>%
  add_model(logistic_model) %>%
  add_recipe(rec)

Running 10_fold cross validation

results <- fit_resamples(
  wf,
  resamples = folds,
  metrics = metric_set(accuracy, roc_auc),
  control = control_resamples(save_pred = TRUE, verbose = FALSE)
)

Collecting the metrics

collect_metrics(results)
## # A tibble: 2 × 6
##   .metric  .estimator  mean     n    std_err .config        
##   <chr>    <chr>      <dbl> <int>      <dbl> <chr>          
## 1 accuracy binary     0.879    10 0.00000488 pre0_mod0_post0
## 2 roc_auc  binary     0.498    10 0.00137    pre0_mod0_post0
preds <- collect_predictions(results)

The average accurcacy is 87.85% and average roc-auc is 49.77% respectively.

Confusion Matrix (2 x 2 Table)

conf_mat(preds, truth = Heart_Attack, estimate = .pred_class)
##           Truth
## Prediction     No    Yes
##        No  147136  20349
##        Yes      0      0

The model fails to predict anyone with heart attack, but accurately predict those without the heart attack.

Model Performance metrics

metrics <- metric_set(accuracy, sens, spec, precision, recall, f_meas)

all_metrics_lr <- metrics(preds,
               truth = Heart_Attack,
               estimate = .pred_class, event_level = "second")
## Warning: While computing binary `precision()`, no predicted events were detected (i.e.
## `true_positive + false_positive = 0`).
## Precision is undefined in this case, and `NA` will be returned.
## Note that 20349 true event(s) actually occurred for the problematic event
## level, Yes
## While computing binary `precision()`, no predicted events were detected (i.e.
## `true_positive + false_positive = 0`).
## Precision is undefined in this case, and `NA` will be returned.
## Note that 20349 true event(s) actually occurred for the problematic event
## level, Yes
kable(all_metrics_lr)
.metric .estimator .estimate
accuracy binary 0.8785026
sens binary 0.0000000
spec binary 1.0000000
precision binary
recall binary 0.0000000
f_meas binary

ROC Plot

roc_auc(preds,
        truth = Heart_Attack,
        .pred_Yes)
## # A tibble: 1 × 3
##   .metric .estimator .estimate
##   <chr>   <chr>          <dbl>
## 1 roc_auc binary         0.502
roc_curve <- preds %>%
  roc_curve(truth = Heart_Attack, .pred_Yes) %>%
  autoplot()

plot(roc_curve)

Coefficient Estimates

final_model <- fit(wf, data = train_data)


fitted_model <- final_model %>% extract_fit_engine()
tidy(fitted_model)
## # A tibble: 39 × 5
##    term                      estimate std.error statistic p.value
##    <chr>                        <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>
##  1 (Intercept)              -1.98       0.00748 -264.      0     
##  2 Age                      -0.00742    0.00748   -0.991   0.322 
##  3 Blood_Pressure            0.00317    0.00748    0.424   0.672 
##  4 CVD_Risk_Score           -0.00770    0.00748   -1.03    0.303 
##  5 Gender_Male              -0.000138   0.00748   -0.0184  0.985 
##  6 Smoking_Status_Smoker    -0.00607    0.00748   -0.811   0.417 
##  7 Diabetes_Yes             -0.00439    0.00751   -0.584   0.559 
##  8 Obesity_Yes               0.00559    0.00747    0.749   0.454 
##  9 Cholesterol_Level_Low    -0.0179     0.00865   -2.07    0.0388
## 10 Cholesterol_Level_Normal -0.00852    0.00861   -0.989   0.322 
## # ℹ 29 more rows

Variables Importance

final_model %>%
  extract_fit_parsnip() %>%
  vip(num_features = 20)

The top 5 variables that are found to be most important in logistic regression are Low cholesterol level, poor diet score, low stress level, middle income level and unemployed.

Testing the final model on testing dataset

test_preds <- predict(final_model, new_data = test_data, type = "class") %>%
  bind_cols(test_data)

Confusion metrics for testing data

conf_mat(test_preds,
         truth = Heart_Attack,
         estimate = .pred_class)
##           Truth
## Prediction    No   Yes
##        No  63059  8722
##        Yes     0     0

The final model fails to predict anyone with heart attack, but accurately predict those without the heart attack.

calculating different metrics for testing data

metrics <- metric_set(accuracy, sens, spec, precision, recall, f_meas)

metrics(test_preds,
        truth = Heart_Attack,
        estimate = .pred_class,
        event_level = "second")
## Warning: While computing binary `precision()`, no predicted events were detected (i.e.
## `true_positive + false_positive = 0`).
## Precision is undefined in this case, and `NA` will be returned.
## Note that 8722 true event(s) actually occurred for the problematic event level,
## Yes
## While computing binary `precision()`, no predicted events were detected (i.e.
## `true_positive + false_positive = 0`).
## Precision is undefined in this case, and `NA` will be returned.
## Note that 8722 true event(s) actually occurred for the problematic event level,
## Yes
## # A tibble: 6 × 3
##   .metric   .estimator .estimate
##   <chr>     <chr>          <dbl>
## 1 accuracy  binary         0.878
## 2 sens      binary         0    
## 3 spec      binary         1    
## 4 precision binary        NA    
## 5 recall    binary         0    
## 6 f_meas    binary        NA

Logistic regression with up- sampling

###Creating the Recipe

Heart_rec_oversamp <- 
  recipe(Heart_Attack ~ ., data = train_data) %>%
  step_rm(Patient_ID, Province, Hypertension) %>%
  step_smotenc(Heart_Attack, over_ratio = 0.9) %>%
  step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors()) %>%
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors()) %>% 
  step_corr(all_numeric_predictors(), threshold = 0.9) %>%
  step_nzv(all_predictors()) 
recipe(~., train_data) %>%
  step_upsample(Heart_Attack, over_ratio = 0.9) %>%
  prep() %>%
  bake(new_data = NULL) %>%
  ggplot(aes(Heart_Attack)) +
  geom_bar()

### Define Logistic Regression

logistic_m <- logistic_reg(
                mode = "classification",
                engine = "glm"
              )

Creating Workflow

HeartAttack_wflow_oversamp <- 
  workflow() %>% 
  add_model(logistic_m) %>% 
  add_recipe(Heart_rec_oversamp)

HeartAttack_wflow_oversamp
## ══ Workflow ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
## Preprocessor: Recipe
## Model: logistic_reg()
## 
## ── Preprocessor ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## 6 Recipe Steps
## 
## • step_rm()
## • step_smotenc()
## • step_dummy()
## • step_normalize()
## • step_corr()
## • step_nzv()
## 
## ── Model ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Logistic Regression Model Specification (classification)
## 
## Computational engine: glm

### Running 10-fold cross validation

Heart_fit_oversamp <- 
  fit_resamples(
    HeartAttack_wflow_oversamp,
    resamples = folds,
    metrics = metric_set(accuracy, roc_auc),
    control = control_resamples(save_pred = TRUE, verbose = FALSE)
  )

Collecting the results

collect_metrics(Heart_fit_oversamp)
## # A tibble: 2 × 6
##   .metric  .estimator  mean     n std_err .config        
##   <chr>    <chr>      <dbl> <int>   <dbl> <chr>          
## 1 accuracy binary     0.558    10 0.00135 pre0_mod0_post0
## 2 roc_auc  binary     0.502    10 0.00188 pre0_mod0_post0

Model performance metrics

preds2 <- collect_predictions(Heart_fit_oversamp)

metrics <- metric_set(accuracy, sens, spec, precision, recall, f_meas)
Heart_fit_oversamp_all_metrics <- metrics(preds2,
                                          truth = Heart_Attack,
                                          estimate = .pred_class,
                                          event_level = "second")
kable(Heart_fit_oversamp_all_metrics)
.metric .estimator .estimate
accuracy binary 0.5579783
sens binary 0.4293086
spec binary 0.5757734
precision binary 0.1227742
recall binary 0.4293086
f_meas binary 0.1909425

ROC Curve

roc_auc(preds2, truth = Heart_Attack, .pred_Yes)
## # A tibble: 1 × 3
##   .metric .estimator .estimate
##   <chr>   <chr>          <dbl>
## 1 roc_auc binary         0.498
preds2 %>%
  roc_curve(truth = Heart_Attack, .pred_Yes) %>%
  autoplot()

Fitting the final model on entire training data

final_model2 <- fit(HeartAttack_wflow_oversamp, data = train_data)


final_model2 %>%
  extract_fit_parsnip() %>%
  tidy() %>%
  arrange(desc(abs(estimate)))
## # A tibble: 39 × 5
##    term                          estimate std.error statistic   p.value
##    <chr>                            <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>
##  1 Previous_Heart_Attack_Yes      -0.211    0.00415    -50.7  0        
##  2 Diabetes_Yes                   -0.195    0.00407    -47.8  0        
##  3 Chronic_Kidney_Disease_Yes     -0.176    0.00397    -44.2  0        
##  4 Air_Pollution_Exposure_Medium   0.133    0.00525     25.4  6.22e-142
##  5 (Intercept)                    -0.115    0.00386    -29.7  9.79e-194
##  6 Family_History_CVD_Yes         -0.110    0.00388    -28.3  7.15e-176
##  7 Obesity_Yes                    -0.0752   0.00387    -19.4  3.02e- 84
##  8 Alcohol_Consumption_Yes        -0.0746   0.00387    -19.3  5.38e- 83
##  9 Air_Pollution_Exposure_Low      0.0687   0.00524     13.1  3.07e- 39
## 10 Rural_or_Urban_Urban           -0.0340   0.00386     -8.82 1.18e- 18
## # ℹ 29 more rows

Variables Importance

final_model2 %>%
  extract_fit_parsnip() %>%
  vip(num_features = 20)

Testing the final model on testing dataset

test_preds2 <- predict(final_model2, new_data = test_data, type = "class") %>%
  bind_cols(test_data)

Confusion metrics for testing data

conf_mat(test_preds2,
         truth = Heart_Attack,
         estimate = .pred_class)
##           Truth
## Prediction    No   Yes
##        No  36145  5057
##        Yes 26914  3665

calculating different metrics for testing data

metrics <- metric_set(accuracy, sens, spec, precision, recall, f_meas)

metrics(test_preds2,
        truth = Heart_Attack,
        estimate = .pred_class,
        event_level = "second")
## # A tibble: 6 × 3
##   .metric   .estimator .estimate
##   <chr>     <chr>          <dbl>
## 1 accuracy  binary         0.555
## 2 sens      binary         0.420
## 3 spec      binary         0.573
## 4 precision binary         0.120
## 5 recall    binary         0.420
## 6 f_meas    binary         0.187

Random Forests

Loading the Libraries

library(tidyverse)
library(tidymodels)
library(sjPlot)
library(C50)
library(ranger)
library(finalfit)
library(knitr)
library(gtsummary)
library(mlbench)
library(vip)
library(rsample)
library(rpart.plot)
## Loading required package: rpart
## 
## Attaching package: 'rpart'
## The following object is masked from 'package:dials':
## 
##     prune
library(tune)
library(recipes)
library(yardstick)
library(parsnip)
library(glmnet)
library(themis)
library(microbenchmark)

Setting the cores for fast computation

### Set the number of cores on your computer
cores <- parallel::detectCores()
cores
## [1] 10

Defining Random forest

rf_model <- rand_forest(mtry = tune(), min_n = tune(), trees = 100) %>% 
              set_engine("ranger", num.threads = cores) %>% 
              set_mode("classification")

Creating the recipe

heartattack_recipe <- 
  recipe(Heart_Attack ~ ., data = train_data) %>%
  step_rm(Patient_ID, Province, Hypertension) %>%  
   step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors()) %>%   
   step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors()) %>%   
  step_corr(all_numeric_predictors(), threshold = 0.9) %>%
  step_nzv(all_predictors())

Creating workflow

heartattack_workflow <- 
        workflow() %>% 
        add_model(rf_model) %>% 
        add_recipe(heartattack_recipe)

heartattack_workflow
## ══ Workflow ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
## Preprocessor: Recipe
## Model: rand_forest()
## 
## ── Preprocessor ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## 5 Recipe Steps
## 
## • step_rm()
## • step_dummy()
## • step_normalize()
## • step_corr()
## • step_nzv()
## 
## ── Model ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Random Forest Model Specification (classification)
## 
## Main Arguments:
##   mtry = tune()
##   trees = 100
##   min_n = tune()
## 
## Engine-Specific Arguments:
##   num.threads = cores
## 
## Computational engine: ranger

creating 10-folds and creating grid to find optimal hyperparameters

set.seed(10)

folds <- vfold_cv(train_data, v = 10) 

rf_grid <- grid_regular(
              mtry(range = c(5, 25)),
              min_n(range = c(5, 50)),
              levels = 5  
            )

rf_grid
## # A tibble: 25 × 2
##     mtry min_n
##    <int> <int>
##  1     5     5
##  2    10     5
##  3    15     5
##  4    20     5
##  5    25     5
##  6     5    16
##  7    10    16
##  8    15    16
##  9    20    16
## 10    25    16
## # ℹ 15 more rows

Running the random forest model

heartattack_fit <- tune_grid(
                heartattack_workflow,
                resamples = folds,
                grid = rf_grid, 
                control = control_resamples(save_pred = TRUE, 
                                                  verbose = FALSE))

heartattack_fit
## # Tuning results
## # 10-fold cross-validation 
## # A tibble: 10 × 5
##    splits                 id     .metrics          .notes           .predictions
##    <list>                 <chr>  <list>            <list>           <list>      
##  1 <split [150736/16749]> Fold01 <tibble [75 × 6]> <tibble [0 × 4]> <tibble>    
##  2 <split [150736/16749]> Fold02 <tibble [75 × 6]> <tibble [0 × 4]> <tibble>    
##  3 <split [150736/16749]> Fold03 <tibble [75 × 6]> <tibble [0 × 4]> <tibble>    
##  4 <split [150736/16749]> Fold04 <tibble [75 × 6]> <tibble [0 × 4]> <tibble>    
##  5 <split [150736/16749]> Fold05 <tibble [75 × 6]> <tibble [0 × 4]> <tibble>    
##  6 <split [150737/16748]> Fold06 <tibble [75 × 6]> <tibble [0 × 4]> <tibble>    
##  7 <split [150737/16748]> Fold07 <tibble [75 × 6]> <tibble [0 × 4]> <tibble>    
##  8 <split [150737/16748]> Fold08 <tibble [75 × 6]> <tibble [0 × 4]> <tibble>    
##  9 <split [150737/16748]> Fold09 <tibble [75 × 6]> <tibble [0 × 4]> <tibble>    
## 10 <split [150737/16748]> Fold10 <tibble [75 × 6]> <tibble [0 × 4]> <tibble>

Graph showing accuracy as a function of min_n and mtry

heartattack_fit %>%
  collect_metrics() %>%
  filter(.metric == "accuracy") %>%
  select(mean, min_n, mtry) %>%
  pivot_longer(min_n:mtry,
    values_to = "value",
    names_to = "parameter"
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(value, mean, color = parameter)) +
  geom_point(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~parameter, scales = "free_x") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Accuracy")

Collect the results

rf_best <- 
  heartattack_fit %>% 
  select_best(metric = "accuracy")

rf_best
## # A tibble: 1 × 3
##    mtry min_n .config         
##   <int> <int> <chr>           
## 1     5     5 pre0_mod01_post0
rf_auc_fit <- 
  heartattack_fit %>% 
  collect_predictions(parameters = rf_best)

The optimal tuning parameters are mtry =5 (each split, only 5 random predictors are considered) and min_n = 5 (each node must have atleast 5 observations to split further).

Confusion matrix

conf_mat(rf_auc_fit, truth = Heart_Attack,
         estimate = .pred_class)
##           Truth
## Prediction     No    Yes
##        No  147136  20349
##        Yes      0      0

The model fails to predict anyone with the heart attack, but perform well to predict those without the heart attack.

Model Performance metrics

metrics2 <- metric_set(accuracy, sens, spec, precision, recall, f_meas)

all_metrics_lr2 <- metrics2(rf_auc_fit,
               truth = Heart_Attack,
               estimate = .pred_class, event_level = "second")
## Warning: While computing binary `precision()`, no predicted events were detected (i.e.
## `true_positive + false_positive = 0`).
## Precision is undefined in this case, and `NA` will be returned.
## Note that 20349 true event(s) actually occurred for the problematic event
## level, Yes
## While computing binary `precision()`, no predicted events were detected (i.e.
## `true_positive + false_positive = 0`).
## Precision is undefined in this case, and `NA` will be returned.
## Note that 20349 true event(s) actually occurred for the problematic event
## level, Yes
kable(all_metrics_lr2)
.metric .estimator .estimate
accuracy binary 0.8785026
sens binary 0.0000000
spec binary 1.0000000
precision binary
recall binary 0.0000000
f_meas binary

ROC-AUC for random forest

auc_result <- roc_auc(
  rf_auc_fit,
  truth = Heart_Attack,
  .pred_Yes,
  event_level = "second"
)
rf_auc_fit %>%
  roc_curve(truth = Heart_Attack, .pred_Yes) %>%
  autoplot()

Final model

final_modelRF <- finalize_model(
                  rf_model,
                  rf_best
                )

final_modelRF
## Random Forest Model Specification (classification)
## 
## Main Arguments:
##   mtry = 5
##   trees = 100
##   min_n = 5
## 
## Engine-Specific Arguments:
##   num.threads = cores
## 
## Computational engine: ranger

Variable Importance

tree_prep <- prep(heartattack_recipe)

final_modelRF %>%
  set_engine("ranger", importance = "permutation") %>%
  fit(Heart_Attack ~ .,
    data = juice(tree_prep)) %>%
  vip(geom = "point")

Fitting the final model using the optimal hyperparameters and testing the peformance on testing data

final_rf_workflow <- workflow() %>%
                      add_recipe(heartattack_recipe) %>%
                      add_model(final_modelRF)

final_resultsRF <- final_rf_workflow %>%
                    last_fit(data_split)

final_resultsRF %>%
  collect_metrics()
## # A tibble: 3 × 4
##   .metric     .estimator .estimate .config        
##   <chr>       <chr>          <dbl> <chr>          
## 1 accuracy    binary         0.878 pre0_mod0_post0
## 2 roc_auc     binary         0.507 pre0_mod0_post0
## 3 brier_class binary         0.108 pre0_mod0_post0

Collecting the predictions

rf_predictions <- final_resultsRF %>%
  collect_predictions()

Confusion matrix based on testing data

rf_predictions %>%
  conf_mat(truth = Heart_Attack, estimate = .pred_class)
##           Truth
## Prediction    No   Yes
##        No  63059  8722
##        Yes     0     0

The model fails to predict anyone with the heart attack, but perform well to predict those without the heart attack.

Metrics for Random forest based on testing data

metricsRF <- metric_set(accuracy, sens, spec, precision, recall, f_meas)

all_metrics_RF <- metricsRF(rf_predictions,
               truth = Heart_Attack,
               estimate = .pred_class, event_level = "second")
## Warning: While computing binary `precision()`, no predicted events were detected (i.e.
## `true_positive + false_positive = 0`).
## Precision is undefined in this case, and `NA` will be returned.
## Note that 8722 true event(s) actually occurred for the problematic event level,
## Yes
## While computing binary `precision()`, no predicted events were detected (i.e.
## `true_positive + false_positive = 0`).
## Precision is undefined in this case, and `NA` will be returned.
## Note that 8722 true event(s) actually occurred for the problematic event level,
## Yes
kable(all_metrics_RF)
.metric .estimator .estimate
accuracy binary 0.8784915
sens binary 0.0000000
spec binary 1.0000000
precision binary
recall binary 0.0000000
f_meas binary

ROC-AUC for random forest

auc_result2 <- roc_auc(
  rf_predictions,
  truth = Heart_Attack,
  .pred_Yes,
  event_level = "second"
)
rf_predictions %>%
  roc_curve(truth = Heart_Attack, .pred_Yes) %>%
  autoplot()

Random forest for up sampling

Setting the cores for fast computation

cores <- parallel::detectCores()
cores
## [1] 10

Creating the Recipe

Heart_rec_oversamp_rf2 <- 
  recipe(Heart_Attack ~ ., data = train_data) %>%
  step_rm(Patient_ID, Province, Hypertension) %>%  
  step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors()) %>%
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors()) %>%
  step_nzv(all_predictors()) %>%
  step_corr(all_numeric_predictors(), threshold = 0.9) %>%
  step_smotenc(Heart_Attack, over_ratio = 0.9)

Defining the Random forest

rf_model <- rand_forest(mtry = tune(), min_n = tune(), trees = 100) %>% 
              set_engine("ranger", num.threads = cores) %>% 
              set_mode("classification")

Creating the workflow

HeartAttack_wflow_oversamp_rf <- 
  workflow() %>%
  add_model(rf_model) %>%
  add_recipe(Heart_rec_oversamp_rf2)

HeartAttack_wflow_oversamp_rf
## ══ Workflow ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
## Preprocessor: Recipe
## Model: rand_forest()
## 
## ── Preprocessor ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## 6 Recipe Steps
## 
## • step_rm()
## • step_dummy()
## • step_normalize()
## • step_nzv()
## • step_corr()
## • step_smotenc()
## 
## ── Model ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Random Forest Model Specification (classification)
## 
## Main Arguments:
##   mtry = tune()
##   trees = 100
##   min_n = tune()
## 
## Engine-Specific Arguments:
##   num.threads = cores
## 
## Computational engine: ranger

Tuning the parameters

rf_grid <- grid_regular(
  mtry(range = c(5, 25)),
  min_n(range = c(5, 50)),
  levels = 5
)

Running the model

rfoversamp_results <- tune_grid(
  HeartAttack_wflow_oversamp_rf,
  resamples = folds,
  grid = rf_grid,
  control = control_resamples(save_pred = TRUE, 
                                                  verbose = FALSE))

Graph showing accuracy as a function of min_n and mtry

rfoversamp_results %>%
  collect_metrics() %>%
  filter(.metric == "accuracy") %>%
  select(mean, min_n, mtry) %>%
  pivot_longer(min_n:mtry,
    values_to = "value",
    names_to = "parameter"
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(value, mean, color = parameter)) +
  geom_point(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~parameter, scales = "free_x") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Accuracy")

Collecting the results

rf_best2 <- 
  rfoversamp_results %>% 
  select_best(metric = "accuracy")

rf_best2
## # A tibble: 1 × 3
##    mtry min_n .config         
##   <int> <int> <chr>           
## 1     5     5 pre0_mod01_post0
rf_auc_fit2 <- 
  rfoversamp_results %>% 
  collect_predictions(parameters = rf_best2)

The optimal tuning parameters are mtry =5 (each split, only 5 random predictors are considered) and min_n = 5 (each node must have atleast 5 observations to split further).

Confusion matrix

conf_mat(rf_auc_fit2, truth = Heart_Attack,
         estimate = .pred_class)
##           Truth
## Prediction     No    Yes
##        No  147136  20348
##        Yes      0      1

Model Performance metrics

metricsRFU <- metric_set(accuracy, sens, spec, precision, recall, f_meas)

all_metrics_RFU <- metricsRFU(rf_auc_fit2,
               truth = Heart_Attack,
               estimate = .pred_class, event_level = "second")
               
kable(all_metrics_RFU)
.metric .estimator .estimate
accuracy binary 0.8785085
sens binary 0.0000491
spec binary 1.0000000
precision binary 1.0000000
recall binary 0.0000491
f_meas binary 0.0000983

ROC-AUC for random forest

auc_result2 <- roc_auc(
  rf_auc_fit2,
  truth = Heart_Attack,
  .pred_Yes,
  event_level = "second"
)
rf_auc_fit2 %>%
  roc_curve(truth = Heart_Attack, .pred_Yes) %>%
  autoplot()

Selecting the best model

final_modelRFU <- finalize_model(
                  rf_model,
                  rf_best2
                )

final_modelRFU
## Random Forest Model Specification (classification)
## 
## Main Arguments:
##   mtry = 5
##   trees = 100
##   min_n = 5
## 
## Engine-Specific Arguments:
##   num.threads = cores
## 
## Computational engine: ranger

Variable Importance

tree_prep2 <- prep(Heart_rec_oversamp_rf2)

final_modelRFU %>%
  set_engine("ranger", importance = "permutation") %>%
  fit(Heart_Attack ~ .,
    data = juice(tree_prep2)) %>%
  vip(geom = "point")

Fitting the final model using the optimal hyperparameters and testing the peformance on testing data

final_rfu_workflow <- workflow() %>%
                      add_recipe(Heart_rec_oversamp_rf2) %>%
                      add_model(final_modelRFU)

final_resultsRFU <- final_rfu_workflow %>%
                    last_fit(data_split)

final_resultsRFU %>%
  collect_metrics()
## # A tibble: 3 × 4
##   .metric     .estimator .estimate .config        
##   <chr>       <chr>          <dbl> <chr>          
## 1 accuracy    binary         0.878 pre0_mod0_post0
## 2 roc_auc     binary         0.497 pre0_mod0_post0
## 3 brier_class binary         0.112 pre0_mod0_post0

Collecting the predictions

rfu_predictions <- final_resultsRFU %>%
  collect_predictions()

Confusion matrix based on testing dataset

rfu_predictions %>%
  conf_mat(truth = Heart_Attack, estimate = .pred_class)
##           Truth
## Prediction    No   Yes
##        No  63057  8722
##        Yes     2     0

Metrics for Random forest based on testing data

metricsRFU <- metric_set(accuracy, sens, spec, precision, recall, f_meas)

all_metrics_RFU <- metricsRFU(rf_predictions,
               truth = Heart_Attack,
               estimate = .pred_class, event_level = "second")
## Warning: While computing binary `precision()`, no predicted events were detected (i.e.
## `true_positive + false_positive = 0`).
## Precision is undefined in this case, and `NA` will be returned.
## Note that 8722 true event(s) actually occurred for the problematic event level,
## Yes
## While computing binary `precision()`, no predicted events were detected (i.e.
## `true_positive + false_positive = 0`).
## Precision is undefined in this case, and `NA` will be returned.
## Note that 8722 true event(s) actually occurred for the problematic event level,
## Yes
kable(all_metrics_RFU)
.metric .estimator .estimate
accuracy binary 0.8784915
sens binary 0.0000000
spec binary 1.0000000
precision binary
recall binary 0.0000000
f_meas binary

ROC-AUC for random forest based on testing dataset

auc_resultRFU <- roc_auc(
  rfu_predictions,
  truth = Heart_Attack,
  .pred_Yes,
  event_level = "second"
)
rfu_predictions %>%
  roc_curve(truth = Heart_Attack, .pred_Yes) %>%
  autoplot()

Discussion

Model

For all four model, similar approach was used, where the data was portioned into training and testing data. 10- fold cross validation was done on the training data to find the hyperparameters and once the best hyperparameters are known final model is fitted on the entire training data and then its predictive performance is tested on the testing data.

Model comparison

Based on confusion matrix, it is apparent that logistic regression, random forest and random forest with up sampling performs well to detect people with no heart attack, but perform worst to detect people with the heart attack. The only model which perform fair in detecting people with heart attack is the logistic regression with up-sampling. The Random forest with up sampling did detect one person with heart disease when the results of cross validation was put into confusion matrix. However when fitted final model was tested on the testing data, it failed to detect any person with heart disease.

The sensitivity and specificity of the logistic regression model with Up-sampling is 0.405 and 0.595 respectively, whereas for other models, the specificity is 1 and the sensitivity is zero. Similar difference can be seen in terms of precision, recall, accuracy and F-measures with logistic regression with up sampling performing better in those parameters. To conclude, logistic regression with up- sampling is the best model among the four, even though its performance is also modest. It is possible that other models such as linear discriminant analysis, quadratic discriminant analysis, naives bayes, bayesian additive regression trees or artificial neural network may perform better than our four models.

Variable importance

Logistic Regression

The top 5 variables that are found to be most important in logistic regression are Low cholesterol level, poor diet score, low stress level, middle income level and unemployed.

Logistic Regression with Up-sampling

The top 5 variables that are found to be most important in logistic regression with up-sampling are Previous heart attack, having diabetes, chronic kidney disease and stroke, Medium air pollution exposure.

Random Forest

The top 5 variables that are found to be most important in random forest are medium air pollution exposure, TCM usage, low air pollution exposure, low cholesterol level and low income level.

Random Forest with Up-sampling

The top 5 variables that are found to be most important in random forest with up-sampling are family history of CVD, TCM usage, alcohol consumption, obesity and being urban residence.

Conclusion

Logistic regression with up-sampling performs fair among the four models. Given the low scores of performance metrics for the logistic regression with up-sampling, it is possible that other classification models such as artifical neural networks, linear or quadratic discriminant analysis, or bayesian classification approaches may perform better in classifying people with heart disaese from people without the heart disease.

References

  1. Kasper D, Fauci A, Hauser S, Longo D, Jameson J, Loscalzo J. Harrison’s principles of internal medicine, 19e. New York, NY, USA:: Mcgraw-hill; 2015.
  2. Salari N, Morddarvanjoghi F, Abdolmaleki A, Rasoulpoor S, Khaleghi AA, Hezarkhani LA, Shohaimi S, Mohammadi M. The global prevalence of myocardial infarction: a systematic review and meta-analysis. BMC cardiovascular disorders. 2023 Apr 22;23(1):206.
